name: Baking
description: Breads, pastries, cakes, and the science of flour, butter, and heat.

questions.q: Which long, thin French bread is a staple of every Paris boulangerie?

source.notes: A Parisian bakery tradition, UNESCO-recognized.

questions.q: What crescent-shaped, buttery French pastry is a classic breakfast item?

source.notes: Literally 'crescent' in French.

questions.q: Which single-celled microorganism makes bread dough rise?

source.notes: Typically Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

questions.q: What ingredient, released when yeast ferments sugar, makes bread rise and gives it an airy texture?

source.notes: CO₂ bubbles get trapped by gluten.

questions.q: Focaccia, a dimpled flatbread often topped with olive oil and herbs, originates in which country?

source.notes: Particularly associated with Genoa and Liguria.

questions.q: What elastic protein, formed when wheat flour is mixed with water, gives bread its chewy structure?

source.notes: Formed from glutenin and gliadin proteins.

questions.q: What chemical leavener, used in quick breads and cookies, is also a cleaning agent?

source.notes: Sodium bicarbonate.

questions.q: Which hollow pastry shell is the basis of éclairs, cream puffs, and profiteroles?

source.notes: Pâte à choux; steam makes it puff.

questions.q: What Italian dessert means 'pick me up' and typically features ladyfingers, espresso, and mascarpone?

source.notes: 'Tirami sù' literally = 'lift me up.'

questions.q: Which popular British TV baking competition is hosted by (among others) Paul Hollywood?

source.notes: Launched on BBC in 2010; Paul Hollywood has been a judge from day one.

questions.q: Which fluffy white Japanese cake rises with whipped egg whites and is often sold in blocks from Harajuku street stands?

source.notes: Originally introduced by Portuguese missionaries in the 16th century.

questions.q: The small colorful French cookies that have gone global in the 21st century, popularized by Pierre Hermé, are called what?

source.notes: Almond-meringue sandwich cookies.

questions.q: Which pastry technique involves repeatedly folding butter into dough to create hundreds of thin layers?

source.notes: Basis of puff pastry, croissants, and Danish.

questions.q: What is the French term for a professional pastry chef?

source.notes: A boulanger is specifically a bread baker.

questions.q: Brioche is a rich French bread known for having a lot of which ingredient?

source.notes: Sometimes contains up to 40% butter by weight.

questions.q: What is added to bread during baking to get a crispier crust on artisan loaves?

source.notes: Professional ovens inject steam in the first few minutes.

questions.q: Which densely rich chocolate-cake-like confection from France is typically served as a small cake made with almond flour?

source.notes: Small rectangular bar-shaped cake, allegedly named after Paris's financial district.

questions.q: Which flaky Austrian/Hungarian dessert typically includes apples, raisins, and cinnamon in paper-thin pastry?

source.notes: Apfelstrudel — Vienna, 17th century.

questions.q: Which sweet, layered Greek pastry uses filo, nuts, and honey syrup?

source.notes: Origin disputed among Greek, Turkish, Lebanese cuisines.

questions.q: Which American-led bakery-show and Food Network host led 'Ace of Cakes' and 'Duff Till Dawn'?

source.notes: Owns Baltimore's Charm City Cakes.

questions.q: Hasselback potatoes — thinly sliced, fan-opened, and roasted — were invented at the Restaurang Hasselbacken in which European capital?

source.notes: Created in Stockholm in the 1950s.

questions.q: Which Ina-Garten-led 'Barefoot Contessa' dessert is essentially layered cocoa-wafer cookies and whipped cream, made famous by Nabisco's Famous Chocolate Wafer?

source.notes: No baking required — softened by fridge time.
